front: Work: the tale of this 'patient' character closes the Decameron; her husband Gualtieri tests her by pretending to kill their children and staging a fake wedding to his 12-year-old daughter
Work: the last of the ten storytellers in this collection has the special right to ignore the day's assigned topic because of his wit
Work: in a story from this collection set in Barbary, the innocent Alibech is seduced into 'putting the Devil back in hell'
Work: in a story from this collection, a Chinese man shames his young rival's plot to outdo his generosity by gladly agreeing to swap identities
Work: in a story from this collection, a judge digs up a venomous toad from a garden to solve the deaths of two lovers who rubbed their teeth with sage
Work: in a story from this collection, eight successive lovers like Uzbek the Turk fail to forestall the virgin wedding of the sultan's daughter Alatiel
Work: in this collection, Saladin tries to trick the Jewish Melchizedek by asking which of the three Abrahamic religions is correct (parable of the three rings)
Work: this collection was dedicated to women who are 'cooped up in the house all day'
Work: this author appears as a character in his own collection, defending it from impropriety charges by telling the story of Filippo Balducci raising his son in a cave
Work: this collection includes a tale where Giotto and Messer Forese da Rabatta make fun of each other after traveling to Northern Tuscany
Work: the story of Gillette of Narbonne in this collection inspired Shakespeare's All's Well That Ends Well
Work: some stories in this collection were adapted from Sanskrit antecedents like the Panchatantra
Work: Tzvetan Todorov analyzed this collection using Freudian theory, identifying conversion and avoiding punishment as two major plot structures
Work: Marguerite of Navarre's Heptameron and Basile's Pentamerone were modeled on this collection
Work: the last tale on the fifth day of this collection (about Pietro de Vionciolo) is adapted from Apuleius's Golden Ass
Work: Christine de Pizan heavily used this collection of biographies of women as a source for The Book of the City of Ladies
back: On Famous Women / De Mulieribus Claris (Giovanni Boccaccio)

front: Work: Chaucer drew material from this collection for his Legend of Good Women
back: On Famous Women / De Mulieribus Claris (Giovanni Boccaccio)

front: Work: this twelve-book poem begins with Theseus besieging the Scythians and marrying Hippolyta; subtitled for the 'nuptials of Emily'
Work: Palemone and Arcita fight over Emily in this poem, adapted by Chaucer as 'The Knight's Tale'
Work: this character, thought to be an alias for Maria d'Aquino, appears across many of Boccaccio's works; receives a bad omen when a flower falls from her crown
Work: this novel by Boccaccio is styled as a confession of the protagonist's love for the merchant Panfilo
back: The Elegy of Lady Fiammetta (Giovanni Boccaccio)

front: Work: this Boccaccio poem tells the story of Troilus and Cressida, inspiring both Chaucer and Shakespeare
Author: Giosue Carducci wrote that this author 'looked outward' while the other two 'three crowns' of Italian literature looked inward and upward
Author: this author was mentored by the Augustinian monk Dionigi, who also mentored Petrarch
Author: this author may have met Chaucer and Petrarch at Violante Visconti's wedding
Author: this author wrote La Caccia di Diana, a catalogue of Neapolitan women
